name: ELVET COLLIERY (CROFTON HOLME X GATE MOOR)

surface_od_ft.qualifier: Ca 300 A.O.D. as written in ink; later pencil '330' (with small query tick) added above the ink figure

units: fathoms/feet/inches, transcribed verbatim — convert ONLY downstream

annotations: 6-inch band between grey sands within a thick drift sequence — possibly a boulder or thin rock rib rather than bedrock
depth fathoms digit heavily inked/overwritten — reads 6, and arithmetic confirms 6-1-7 (36 ft 0 in + 1 ft 7 in = 37 ft 7 in)
small ink scribble/blot in the blank centre of the row, not text
'ramble' = Durham colliery vernacular for loose/broken stone

intervals.verbatim: Dark grey ramble, with black dant
annotations: 'ramble' = loose/broken (argillaceous) stone; 'dant' = soft sooty inferior coal — carbonaceous material, classed mudstone as the dominant lithology

intervals.verbatim: Grey sandstone ramble, with coarse sand
annotations: 'ramble' = loose/broken stone; sits within a water-bearing sand sequence — possibly reworked drift material

intervals.verbatim: Black dant, with coarse sand partings
annotations: 'dant' = soft sooty inferior coal (Durham vernacular); no seam name given; 8 ft 6 in thick with sand partings inside a thick water-bearing sand sequence — likely redeposited coaly material in buried-valley drift, NOT a workable seam; class follows the vernacular word, treat with caution downstream

intervals.verbatim: Brown sand, with thin dant partings
annotations: thin dant (soft coaly) partings within the sand

intervals.verbatim: Dark grey sand, with clay partings

annotations: thickness feet digit slightly overstruck but reads 5; arithmetic confirms (163 ft 6 in + 5 ft 0 in = 168 ft 6 in = 28-0-6)

intervals.verbatim: Dark grey sand, with stony partings

annotations: entry ends mid-phrase ('into') — boring terminated in brown clay; the continuation sheet (page 2) is ruled but blank
pencil right of description: best reading '201'' i.e. 201 feet — a later checker's total-depth jotting (33-3-0 fathoms = 201 ft exactly); reading of the scrawl is probable, not certain

notes: Sheet title (handwritten): 'SECTION OF borehole near Crofton Holme, Crossgate Moor' — the hand could also be read 'Croften Holme' / 'Crossgale Moor' but Crofton Holme / Crossgate Moor are the attested place names. Header: NGR NZ 2587 4308 (matches the index grid reference); Surface Level 'Ca 300 A.O.D.' in ink with a later pencil '330' (query tick) above it; Communicated by 'B & S. 2650' (Borings & Sinkings volume ref); Date of boring or sinking 1901; One-inch Map 26; Six-inch Map Durham 26 NE. Corner carries a struck-through '22 over 5' sheet-numbering scribble. Page 2 is a blank ruled continuation sheet with no entries — noted and skipped. CLERK ARITHMETIC: all 25 rows cross-check exactly (previous depth + thickness = stated depth); no slips. NO NAMED COAL SEAMS and no pencil seam correlations — the whole section is superficial: a deep buried-valley drift sequence (clays, water-bearing sands, laminated clay) to 201 ft, never reaching solid Coal Measures. Vernacular: 'ramble' = loose/broken stone, 'dant' = soft sooty inferior coal; the 'Black dant' at interval 15 and the sandstone/ramble bands (7, 12-14) sit inside water-bearing sands and are probably reworked/boulder material — classed by their verbatim lithology with cautionary annotations. Final depth 33-3-0 fathoms = 201 ft = 61.26 m, matching the BGS index depth 61.26 m.
